title: Rigid heddle painted warp scarf
subtitle: Painting a cotton or silk warp before weaving to create a ikat-like colour effect
excerpt: Paint a warp with fibre-reactive dye before threading the rigid heddle loom to produce a softly blurred, ikat-inspired colour effect in the woven scarf. The warp painting takes about an hour; the weaving is plain weave and suitable for beginners who have already warped a rigid heddle loom once.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional warp-painting practice. Painted warps for rigid heddle looms are documented in contemporary British and American weaving teaching as an intermediate-level colour technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A warp that has been coloured with dye in a deliberate pattern before the weaving begins. The dye-to-weave sequence means the colour lands in a slightly unpredictable way in the woven cloth, producing soft blurred transitions between colour zones.

A resist-dyeing technique in which portions of the warp (or weft) are tied and dyed before weaving, so the dyed sections create a pattern in the finished cloth. Painted warp is an informal, non-resist version of the same idea.
The simplest interlacement, used here as the woven structure so the painted warp colour shows clearly without being obscured by a pattern weave.

content.text: scarf uses colour applied before weaving, rather than woven colour change, to create a loosely
-inspired result. Because the weft is a plain neutral thread in
, it recedes and the warp colour reads clearly across the cloth surface.

attrs.title: Dye safety
attrs.body: Wear gloves and apron when applying dye. Work outdoors or with good ventilation. Keep dyed materials away from food surfaces.

items.name: Foam brushes or syringes for applying dye

content.text: Step 1: Measure and pre-wet the warp'

Measure your warp on the warping board to the full planned length but do not thread the loom yet. Immerse the wound warp in soda ash solution (following the dye manufacturer's instructions) for 15 minutes, then squeeze out excess moisture. Lay the damp warp flat on a plastic-covered surface.

Apply your three or four dye colours in blocks along the length of the warp using foam brushes or dye syringes. For a blurred ikat look, allow the colour zones to bleed slightly into each other at the boundaries. For sharper colour blocks, apply carefully to the centre of each zone and keep a small undyed gap between colours.

Roll the painted warp in plastic wrap and leave to cure at room temperature for at least 8 hours (or overnight). Unwrap, rinse in cold water until the water runs clear, then wash gently in warm soapy water. Dry the warp thoroughly before threading the loom.

Thread the dried painted warp onto the rigid heddle loom at 20 epi in a standard 10-dent rigid heddle. The colour zones in the warp will align in the scarf; how they align depends on the warp width and how many warp threads span each colour zone. Weave in plain weave with a neutral or white weft.

items.problem: The dye washed out almost completely and the warp is pale
items.cause: The soda ash pre-treatment was insufficient or the cure time was too short
items.fix: Fibre-reactive dyes require soda ash to fix permanently to cotton. If the warp is already woven, the colour cannot be restored. For the next batch, use the full soda ash soak and a minimum 8-hour cure time.

items.problem: The colour looks muddy where two colours met rather than cleanly blended
items.cause: The two colours were complementary colours that mixed to brown when wet
items.fix: Use nearby colours on the colour wheel (blue next to green, red next to orange). Opposite colours mix to brown where zones meet. Choose adjacent colours and the boundaries will blend rather than muddy.
